questions.question: What datasets did 'An Image is Worth 16x16 Words: Transformers for Image Recognition at Scale' use?
questions.system_answer: The paper used ImageNet, ImageNet-21k, and JFT-300M for pre-training. It also reports transfer/evaluation results on ImageNet, ImageNet-Real, CIFAR-100, and the VTAB suite of 19 tasks.

questions.question: What is the parameter count of the largest variant in 'Training data-efficient image transformers & distillation through attention'?
questions.gold_answer: The largest DeiT variant is DeiT-B★ (DeiT-B with distillation) at 87M parameters.
questions.system_answer: The largest variant listed is DeiT-B★, which has 87M parameters. The paper’s reference vision transformer is 86M, but the biggest variant in the provided evidence is 87M.

questions.question: What architectural innovations does 'Swin Transformer: Hierarchical Vision Transformer using Shifted Windows' introduce?
questions.system_answer: The paper introduces a hierarchical vision Transformer that computes self-attention inside non-overlapping local windows, then alternates with shifted window partitioning to create cross-window connections. It also uses patch merging to build multi-scale feature maps, giving linear computational complexity with respect to image size; the evidence also notes relative position bias as a beneficial design choice.

questions.question: Briefly describe the architecture of 'Emerging Properties in Self-Supervised Vision Transformers'.
questions.gold_answer: DINO is a self-supervised vision transformer training method
questions.system_answer: The paper presents DINO, a simple self-supervised Vision Transformer architecture based on self-distillation without labels: a student network is trained to match a momentum-teacher network across multiple augmented views, with centering and sharpening used to prevent collapse. It is designed to work with both ViTs and convnets, but the paper emphasizes that ViTs with small patches benefit especially strongly.

questions.question: How many papers in the corpus mention or benchmark on ImageNet?
questions.system_answer: The corpus contains 51 papers that mention ImageNet and 72 papers that benchmark on ImageNet.

questions.question: Which venue has the most papers in this corpus?
questions.system_answer: Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition appears most frequently across the corpus, with 22 papers.

questions.question: How many unique canonical datasets appear across all 100 papers?
questions.gold_answer: There are over 600 unique canonical datasets across the 100 papers.
questions.system_answer: 608 distinct canonical datasets are mentioned across the corpus of 100 papers.

questions.question: How many papers in this corpus were published in 2022?
questions.system_answer: 21 papers in the corpus were published in 2022.

questions.question: How many papers in the corpus use both self-attention and patch embedding?
questions.system_answer: 10 papers in the corpus use both self-attention and patch embedding.

questions.question: Do papers in this corpus agree on top-1 accuracy on ImageNet?
questions.gold_answer: No—reported top-1 ImageNet accuracy in this corpus varies widely from 1.3% to 91.0% across 594 results.
questions.system_answer: No. The corpus reports a very wide range of ImageNet top-1 accuracies, from 1.3 to 91.0 (spread 89.7), so the papers do not agree on a single value. There are also conflicting SOTA claims: CoCa (finetuned) is listed at 91.0, while other papers report lower top claims such as PaLI-17B at 90.9, MaxViT-XL at 88.7/88.51, and MaxViT-L at 86.7/85.17.

questions.question: What is the range of top-1 accuracy values reported on CIFAR-100 in this corpus?
questions.gold_answer: The top-1 accuracy on CIFAR-100 in this corpus ranges from 48.5% to 91.88% across 27 results.
questions.system_answer: Across the corpus, reported CIFAR-100 top-1 accuracy values range from 48.5% to 91.88%, a spread of 43.38 percentage points. The evidence does not list any explicit SOTA claims for CIFAR-100, though the highest reported value is 91.88% (AdaptFormer-Swin).

questions.question: Do papers in this corpus agree on which model holds SOTA on ADE20K mIoU?
questions.gold_answer: No—this corpus does not agree on a single ADE20K mIoU SOTA model, since 7 papers each claim SOTA on the benchmark.
questions.system_answer: No. The corpus contains conflicting SOTA claims for ADE20K mIoU: InternImage-H is listed at 62.9, while ViT-Adapter-L is also repeatedly claimed as SOTA at 61.5. Other strong results include SwinV2-G at 59.9, but the evidence does not show a single agreed-upon state-of-the-art model.

questions.question: Do papers in this corpus agree on mIoU values on ADE20K?
questions.gold_answer: No—reported ADE20K mIoU values vary widely across 97 results, from 26.6 to 62.9.
questions.system_answer: No. The corpus shows a wide spread of reported ADE20K mIoU values, from 26.6 up to 62.9 (stddev 7.29), so the papers do not agree on a single value. There are also conflicting SOTA claims: InternImage-H is reported at 62.9, while ViT-Adapter-L claims 61.5 and SwinV2-G 59.9, indicating different papers report different top results.

questions.question: Do papers in this corpus agree on whether position embeddings are necessary for vision transformers?
questions.gold_answer: Papers disagree on the necessity of position embeddings; some ablations show minimal impact while others demonstrate clear performance drops without them.
questions.system_answer: No. The corpus shows disagreement: some papers treat position embeddings as standard or necessary for ViTs, e.g. ViT says it "adds position embeddings" and CPVT says positional encodings are used to "incorporate the order of sequences" and that ViTs "follow the same fashion". But other papers report they are not essential or can be removed with little/no loss, such as CvT: "Removing position embedding of CvT does not degrade performance," and the self-supervised ViT study says removing them "only slightly hurts performance."
